speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three. Pardon me. Mr. Ziegler, I just want to ask you a question. When you had the hard rations with those blue cards, what did those rations consist of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Primarily bread, potatoes, some margarine, and occasionally a vegetable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got all this food, were you able to share it with anybody else, like any of your, I don't know, friends? Or could you get it to your family? Any of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the family, we all shared. We all shared. Every one that worked received those rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean when you got the hard rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the hard rations? Hard rations consisted of more than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I'm asking. What were the hard rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I also received some sugar. I received some meat, like sausage, and occasionally even fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you share that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. I brought it home. We still lived as a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Very good. OK. So let's go back now. So here you were. Could you just tell me a little bit about the-- the camp that you were in just before you went off to the-- to your first labor camp? You said you were in a-- in a small camp. What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brande.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before Brande. Before Brande. Weren't you in a [INAUDIBLE] somewhere before Brande?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was no camp before Brande. Brande was the first camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a camp, but weren't you-- you said you were in some sort of a barrack or some sort of a camp, in some sort of-- you were held somewhere for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was only for a week before, when they picked me up from my home. When I was sick, I did not appear to work. They picked me up. They sent me-- that was a holding camp or jail, whatever you want to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was just-- it was called [GERMAN] or holding camps for--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And what were the conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. You did not work. You were just sitting around, waiting till they will pick you up and send you to-- to a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people get shot or beaten there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There you only stayed for a few days at most, a holding place. Unless you tried to escape or something. That's when you would get shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went to Brande.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Brande.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how long were you in Brande?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brande, I was there from October '42 until the end of May '43. In Brande were selections every few weeks, three to four weeks. There used to come a civilian, Nazi, which we nicknamed The Horse Trader. He was trading human lives. He was picking up all the healthy ones and shipping them out to various forced labor camps. Those that were not healthy or in physical condition, would send them to one of the extermination camps, which was mostly Auschwitz, to be gassed. And the others, like I mentioned before, were killed right in Brande, that were extremely sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were doing this work. And you were getting your blue cards.

speaker: interviewee
text: The blue cards I was getting when I worked in the glass house, before I was in the camp, not in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. So what were you doing in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, the first months or so, I worked cleaning up the construction sites where previously they were building the Reis Autobahn, the superhighways. Then I was chosen into the burial brigade to bury the dead, which we had, every week, about a dozen to bury. So we go twice weekly or so, load up the wagon with the dead, take them to the neighboring town, which was called Falkenberg. There was an old Jewish cemetery in town with Jewish headstones. It was pretty well full. So we filled up that cemetery. And then we were burying the remainder of the dead on top of the old graves. You only dug out four to five feet and put the corpses in on top of the old graves. Within a couple of months-- by about 10th of March, April-- we had completely filled up that cemetery with the dead. Then the last months-- like in April, May when I was there-- we buried the other dead in a clearing in the forest beside the camp, taking them out and burying them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no markers, no monuments?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were no humans. In Brande, I remember burying two of my Jewish friends. One's name was Korszu Gershon. And other, I don't remember even his first name. Kava was the second. Now, Kava was the son of a wealthy Jew, the son of an engineer. His father was employed in the huge steel mill, the only Jew that worked in that steel mill. I believe they were French citizens. And most of the heavy industry was foreign owned in Poland, like the coal mines and the steel mills, either French, Italian, German, and so forth. He was the son of him, Kava. He died in our camp. He was brought in from another camp very sick, and he was dead within a couple of days. I remember burying him and burying the other fellow, Korszu, which was a neighbor of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after this? After Brande, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we-- I was-- every time there came a section, I was sent away. I was considered too young to go to a forced labor camp yet. They used to call me [GERMAN], the guy that was making the selection, the Nazi-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], disappear, you little runt or whatever, he'd say, you know, send me back. So I went back, and I worked inside the camp. As they say, you get used to any conditions. The human being is stronger than any animal, which you've seen death before your eyes on a daily basis-- killing and so-- and you almost become immune to it. It doesn't bother you anymore. But the will to survive stays with you. Otherwise, you'll never make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we drop Brande, can you describe the buildings there, the barracks, and the living conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks were regular barracks. They were about-- oh, I would say-- I would say about 100 feet by about 35 feet. And each barrack housed about 200 prisoners. There were about six barracks there for them  to stay. There was a kitchen and an eating area in the kitchen, an eating room or a dining room if you want to call it, for like very primitive wooden benches and things. And there was a shower wall where you were going in to take the showers every morning. And there were outhouses, like toilets, outside. The barracks were extremely cold. There was bunk beds in that barrack. There were one prisoner per each-- like bunk beds which were two, one on top of the other-- double bunk beds-- and one for each bunk bed. Now, the bunk bed had to made to perfection. There were bags of straw in the bunk beds. like for a mattress, which you had to try to spread it when you got up. And you had only five minutes to do that. Then stretch the blanket over it. And you had a bench or a stool in front of every bunk bed. So you'd take the-- you would take out two boards from the bunk bed. Underneath the straw back were boards to support the back of the mattress thing. And wedge in the blanket from one end of the bunk bed and the other. Stretch it tight, and then go over with the stool on top of the bunk bed to make it as flat as the bunk bed, almost like it was ironed out to perfection. If you didn't make the bunk bed right, you received 25 lashes. And the whole room, the whole barracks, would have to do exercise. And our exercise-- running around the barracks up and down, up and down-- that was the punishment for it. And it had to be spotlessly cleaned up, no straw found or anything, because the inspection was-- when it was roll call in the morning, right after was the inspection of the barracks, how they are. That was the life-- the daily life. Any beatings and things were daily. Two guys were caught once stealing potatoes. They were buried alive up to their necks, just a head sticking out of the ground, and two bowls of soup put in front of their eyes. We went out to work. When we came back from work, we were marched past them to show us, this is the punishment for things. When they were taken out, they were half dead. They were 12 hours in the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of thoughts went through your mind on a daily basis? How were you able to survive? You've gotten beaten, whacked over the head.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] it went through my mind that I have to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: In order to tell the world of what happened. Because it was unbelievable or incomprehensible that people could imagine something like that, that that is possible that-- of what one human being can do to another. I pictured myself that if somebody would tell me a story like this, I would not want to believe them. So therefore, I made it almost a vow if I-- to survive and to be able. This was my way, almost, of fighting back, is to show them that I will survive, that I can make it. This was my resistance, not to give in.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Brande, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sent, eventually-- it was in May, late in May, where I was eventually chosen to be sent to a forced labor camp, a regular forced labor camp. That camp was called Graditz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Graditz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was in the vicinity of Breslau, not far from Breslau. This camp was located in a former flour mill. It must have been about 10,000 square feet of floor space for where we were sleeping. There were crammed in close to 3,000 people in it. We were sleeping on shelves, three shelves, just like sardines in a can, squeezed in one next to the other. If one turned, he pushed against the other one. The conditions of living as far as torture and beating wasn't as cruel and as hard as was Brande. But the hygiene conditions were much worse, to the extreme. There, you dreaded summer, because, in the hot months like July and August, the stench and the heat-- you could hardly breathe. The dirt was extreme. You had lice. You had fleas. You had bed bugs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as long as you were there, you kept on scratching at the bites. People were going around with open sores, rotting away alive, going to work like this. Eventually came selections, sick once again sent to the extermination camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was put to work there at the river, regulating or shoring up the river. So the first thing they put me is to split rocks, like limestone was there. We're splitting the rocks in order-- to smaller pieces to shore up the river. And then put topsoil over it and drive piles into it and make rows, like from small-- from branches and small trees, you know, rows to hold down the ground so it wouldn't wash away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was put to split the rock. I was given a sledgehammer. Being that I come from a mining town, I was handy with those things. I knew it because, as a child, I used to go with my Polish friends whose parents were unemployed, former coal miners. They were digging coal illegally in the fields on their own. So I used to go during the summer months, holidays. I used to go with them during the day, jump into the hole, pick up a pick, or sometimes we found there iron ore, splitting it with the hammer. So we knew that the rock forms layers where it's formed, you know. And you have to hit it a certain way in order to split it. So it was nothing new to me. I put on the rock. And with one blow, I would split it. The supervisor or foreman on the job was a Nazi, an extremely cruel man, walking around like with baseball bat-- a bat in his hand, hitting left and right people. You lazy bums and so forth, you know, cursing and hitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: The majority of people either were professional or small tradesmen or business-- small business people. He'd never held a sledgehammer or a pick or a shovel in his hand. Of course, he didn't know how to work. He found it very difficult, and he was on top of it. He was beaten, many times killed at work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he stops at me, watched me, a youngster-- let's say of 15-- splitting the rock. With every blow, I split it. So he tell me to stop and calls me over. He says, what's your name? First thing he asked me. So I tell him. Where did you learn to handle a hammer like this? The next question. So I says, well, sir, I come from a mining town. I used to go out where the people used to pick coal and iron ore as a child, sometimes picking up, as a youngster, the hammer. Oh, that's why you are so smart. OK, come with me. You don't need to do that work. You show those lazy bums how to handle the hammer and how to handle the pick.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So I go over to the men-- they're grown men-- and I show them how to pick up the hammer. I say, when you pick it up, put your hand-- the left hand to the end, the other one as close to the hammer itself as possible. That reduces the weight. You lift it. When you drop it, just let your hand glide down. Don't hold it stiff. The weight of it brings it down by itself. With a little push, it'll do the work. Now, put up the stone like this. Make a hollow underneath it, where you blow, and look how it's grown. And hit it in such a spot. And he's standing with a smirk on his face. A youngster-- a kid-- explaining to the men how to work and how to handle, you know, the law of gravity, and think, you know, explaining to men. So the smirk. Next thing to show is to show them with the pick, the guys that are digging there. It's the same thing. I says, when you hit with the pick, don't pull the pick back. Just leverage, lift up the handle. The leverage of the pick will break off the chunk. By pulling here, you are using your muscles. I says, try to eliminate the use of the muscles as much as possible. You don't work as hard, and you create more work. I was explaining them the detail, almost like the scientific works of a thing which I was always interested in learning how to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I am becoming his pet. Wherever he goes, I have to follow him. We are building a rail to bring in the-- the stone and thing, like in carts. So I am setting up. I have the wrenches, the rails, everything. [? Heinrich ?] comes, do you know how to handle a saw? Yes, Sir. Let me see. He takes a small tree trunk, put it down on the horses. Cut off a piece three feet long for a pile to drive into the ground. So I put it down, and hand extending in a cut. He says, the other guy cuts it in the middle. Why do you start cutting at the end of it? I says, if I cut in the middle-- I says, you couldn't cut it either in the middle, because it will bind it. I says, at the end, the weight brings it down. The opening becomes larger, and it's easy to cut. He says, you've got the answer for everything, haven't you, he says, and he starts laughing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Comes lunch time, he goes over to the cook there. Give him an extra bowl of soup, which was what they called [GERMAN]. He's the best worker in that crowd here that you've got. Those lazy bums, they don't deserve nothing. Give him an extra bowl of soup. I have already two bowls of soup, and I don't work. That was my luck for surviving. I worked there for a couple months-- two, maybe three months. Once we come home from work, again a selection for another work group to send out. They select a group of 50. I am among them. I feel disappointed. I am almost sick. I says, here I found already with that murderer, I am his pet. He doesn't bother me anymore. I get along with him tremendously, and I get extra food. Now they are sending me to a different place. I didn't know that the place they were sending me was almost paradise. I was sent to work for a company called Organisation Todt, which was the back up front. It was a warehouse where they stored old and new clothing. That clothing was sorted out, repackaged, and sent to various warehouses in German cities for German nationals that were bumped out during the war. They were given new clothing. So we were like a gathering warehouse and shipping it from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they chose already from the group of 50 29. The rest were unsuited for work. So I hope I'll go back, be sent back. One of the Germans points out to the chief, looks at me and says, what do you think? We take that youngster with us. He looks pretty shrewd. And besides, we don't need him for unloading the trains. We put him with the girls to work, he says, packaging, which he can do. He says, yeah. You are right. Let's take him. They took me. I was sick over it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, they put me to work with the women. And soon enough, I learned the tricks. There is a chance to steal. If I steal a pair of pants and take it into the camp, I give it to one of the prisoners that works with free laborers in a factory somewhere. He can trade those pants for bread. If he receives a loaf of bread, half a loaf was for me. I won't be going hungry much more. I start working there. I was always very good at figures, counting. I was a lightning. Mentally, I could figure, look at things, and just add it up in my mind. So when I was packaging, the German comes in daily and taking-- making out the report on it, counting up, you know, how many we have. And I have one glance. And I see that he made a mistake, put [INAUDIBLE]. So I just walk over to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say, chief-- we used to call him-- chief, I believe there may be a mistake in those figures. Now, I counted it in my mind. And I counted so much. Of course, you must be right, I say to him. But do you mind if we count it over? So he stops and looks at me for a long time. He says, you are as shrewd as a fox, he says. You protect your behind already, that in case you are wrong-- and I'm sure you'll be right-- so you say that I must be right of course. And you probably made a mistake, got it? OK, let's count it. Let's throw it apart. Of course, I was right. So he calls in the big boss and tells him exactly what transpired here, how I explained him everything. And he says to him, what do you  say we let him do the counting? The girls can handle that by themselves, the packaging. We don't need him for that. We put him to counting.

speaker: interviewee
text: From then on, I walked around with a clipboard in my hand and pencil. The transports were coming in. I did the counting off. Now, it may be easy work, but it can be also nerve wracking. If you stand for two, three hours, constantly counting, your head starts to go in circles. And the chief boss, which was a Nazi, [NON-ENGLISH], he used to watch me sometimes from the back when I was counting.

speaker: interviewer
text: I etched a number 99. He would jump and say halt, like stop. And to me, Ziegler, how much have you got? I says, 99, sir. How come? I have 100. I says, you must be right, sir, of course. But that's what I have. I have 99. He turns around with this-- he turns around with a smirk on his face, and he says, continue, walking away. He didn't sway me, catch me. That's tricks like he played on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I worked there till about January, 1944. Eventually, an epidemic broke out in the camp, typhus fever, which was to be expected with the hygienic conditions-- the dirt, the lice, all this. About a week prior to it, I was getting headaches, getting feverish. I was losing my appetite in the camp. I couldn't eat. But people were always hungry, could bite into a rock. The last day of work, it was a Saturday. This time, they called us in especially that Saturday to work. Usually we didn't work Saturdays. They called us in that Saturday to unload a load of coal that came in by train-- train car. And it was a miserable day, snow and rain, wet. And my head was spinning already. I could hardly see straight. The day ended. Two friends of mine were dragging me, practically, by the arms. Because it was six kilometers to-- to the camp back from where we worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got home. I go to the infirmary there. They had a doctor there. They had no medication or anything, also a prisoner doctor. He said, what's wrong with you? So I said, I don't know. I don't feel good. I have very bad headaches and feel cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he just looks in my eyes, and he says, you've got fever. OK, I'll give you-- he had aspirins. I'll give you two aspirins. And he gave me a special permission to stay in camp, not to go down to roll call in the morning, to anywhere, to stay in the-- on the shelf to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: The following night, I could hardly move. He says, come back tomorrow night. How you feel? So my friends took me down again to the infirmary. He took my temperature, and he just whistled. He says, you are staying tomorrow in camp. That tomorrow became almost three weeks. For two weeks, I was in a coma. I remember, even, episodes from the high fever where I got up, put on my pants, and took my knapsack that I had, which you carried always and your pot for the soup. And the orderly there, what the hell is wrong with you? Where are you going? I said, I'm going home. He says, you idiot. You have a fever. You don't know what you're doing. Where-- where has a dog a home? He says to me. [LAUGHS] You have no home. That's your home. And just threw me back on the rotten straw where we were laying, you know. And I remember like they were pulling down the-- one died next to me. So they just grabbed him by the legs, pulled him off. And a new one came in. Then from other side, too. So I was thinking like in a dream. I says, when are they going to pull me off by the legs? Anyways, I regained consciousness.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with consciousness comes hunger. The hunger after typhus fever is just indescribable. I have seen people go literally mad from hunger. So I received my ration of bread. I tried to bite into it. I cannot bite bit into it. Every tooth in my jaws is loose. I am just a skeleton, skin and bone. Even the eyes have a wild look in them. The hair was, already after three weeks, a little bit grown, standing up like on a porcupine. So I was-- remember tearing off a small piece of bread, the inside, stuffing it into my mouth and just sucking on it till it dissolved in my mouth to swallow it. I was lying there for several days. Finally, they take me off, send me back to my room upstairs where we slept on the shelf. My friends see me. They claim the next day, there is a selection that all healthy ones go to a work camp. Roman, you better come with us, because there are rumors that they are going to blow up that camp, to put dynamite under and then pour some petrol over and burn it down. Blow it up and burn it down with all the sick ones, to stop the epidemic. I says, no way. I'm not going with you. If they blow up the camp, I will go fast. I'll be killed. If I go back with you to a forced labor camp, it will be a slow death. I will live-- last a week. It will take me a week to die.
